patches.rationale: EXP-244 W010-F1: Replace Chapman 1933 with canonical Chapman 1919 + Y&M 2017 + Kim & Chang 2018. Chapman 1933 is a lunar tidal paper; Chapman 1919 is the founding Sq framework paper.

patches.find: known since Chapman described it in 1933
patches.rationale: EXP-244 W010-TE1: Sq framework established in 1919, not 1933.

patches.find: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism, documented since 1933 and quantitatively confirmed by Kim & Chang (2018) across 39 eclipses
patches.replace: The Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework, documented since Chapman (1919) and quantitatively confirmed across 39 eclipses by Kim & Chang (2018)
patches.rationale: EXP-244 W010-TV1: Canonical year and name correction.

patches.find: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first described by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering conductivity and altering the Sq current system. Chapman's original work was semi-quantitative; detailed magnitude predictions came from later studies and modern ionospheric models.
patches.replace: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations follow directly from the Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework, first formulated by Chapman (1919, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118) to describe the solar and lunar diurnal variations of terrestrial magnetism. During an eclipse, the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-region conductivity and suppressing the Sq current system; the comprehensive modern review of Sq conductivity is Yamazaki & Maute (2017, Space Science Reviews 206:299–405). Chapman's framework was semi-quantitative at origin; detailed magnitude predictions come from the modern Sq literature and ionospheric models.
patches.rationale: EXP-244 W010-DE1: Chapman 1919 with full venue + Yamazaki & Maute 2017 as modern treatment. Removes mis-attributed 1933 reference.

patches.find: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism — first described by Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) and quantitatively confirmed in the modern era by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054)
patches.replace: The Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework — first formulated by Chapman (1919, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 218:1–118), with modern conductivity treatment in Yamazaki & Maute (2017, Space Science Reviews 206:299–405) — and quantitatively confirmed in the modern era by Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2049)
patches.rationale: EXP-244 W010-DV1: Chapman 1919 + Y&M 2017 + Kim & Chang page range 2054→2049.

patches.find: Standard geophysics supplies the shared cause: East Antarctic crustal roots plus the uncompensated sub-lithospheric mantle plume (Pappa et al., 2019, JGR) produce both the crustal gravity low via isostatic imbalance and, through the associated lithospheric/mantle structure, contribute to the geometry of the SAA field minimum. The co-location of the two anomaly fields is a downstream consequence of common-cause geology, not a novel forecast.
patches.replace: Standard geophysics supplies the shared regional context but resolves it into two distinct proximate mechanisms, not a single shared cause. The Antarctic gravity low is explained by East Antarctic crustal roots plus the uncompensated sub-lithospheric mantle plume acting on crustal density structure via isostatic imbalance (Pappa et al., 2019, JGR). The South Atlantic Anomaly is independently explained as a core-dynamo phenomenon — reverse-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary documented in the CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model (Finlay et al., 2020), and attributed to long-lived heterogeneity at the base of the mantle (the African Large Low Shear Velocity Province) by Tarduno et al. (2015). The two anomalies share a region of deep geological complexity — the African LLSVP footprint influences both overlying lithospheric structure and core-surface flow — but have distinct physical causes, a lithospheric/mantle density feature on one side and a core-surface flux feature on the other, and the dome supplies an equation for neither. The co-location of the two fields is a downstream consequence of shared regional geology, not a novel forecast.
patches.rationale: EXP-245: Split Pappa 2019 overcite — Pappa for gravity anomaly only; add Finlay et al. 2020 (CHAOS-7) + Tarduno et al. 2015 (LLSVP) for SAA. Expected defense_survives 4→2.
